expectations.text: Lists DB_HOST environment variable for database host
expectations.evidence: Section 1 lists 'DB_HOST' with Description: 'The hostname or IP address of your PostgreSQL database server', Example: 'postgres.example.com or 10.0.1.50'

expectations.text: Lists DB_PORT environment variable for database port
expectations.evidence: Section 2 lists 'DB_PORT' with Description: 'The port number on which PostgreSQL is listening', Example: '5432 (default PostgreSQL port)'

expectations.text: Lists DB_NAME environment variable for database name
expectations.evidence: Section 3 lists 'DB_NAME' with Description: 'The name of the PostgreSQL database to connect to', Example: 'shopdb_prod or onlineshop_production'

expectations.text: Lists DB_USERNAME environment variable for database username
expectations.evidence: Section 4 lists 'DB_USERNAME' with Description: 'PostgreSQL database username with access to the database', Example: 'shopuser_prod'

expectations.text: Lists DB_PASSWORD environment variable for database password
expectations.evidence: Section 5 lists 'DB_PASSWORD' with Description: 'Password for the database user account', Example: 'A strong, randomly-generated password'

expectations.text: Lists JWT_SECRET environment variable for token signing
expectations.evidence: Section 6 lists 'JWT_SECRET' with Description: 'Base64-encoded secret key used to sign and verify JWT tokens', Purpose: 'Ensures JWT tokens cannot be forged; critical for authentication security'

Section 7 lists 'CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS' with Description: 'Comma-separated list of frontend URLs allowed to make cross-origin requests', Example: 'https://shop.example.com,https://admin.shop.example.com'

expectations.text: Explains what each environment variable is used for
expectations.evidence: Each variable has 'Purpose' and 'Used in' fields explaining its role. For example, DB_HOST Purpose: 'Used to construct the JDBC connection URL', JWT_SECRET Purpose: 'Ensures JWT tokens cannot be forged; critical for authentication security', Used in: 'app.jwt.secret (mapped to JwtProperties record)'
JWT_SECRET section states 'Type: String (Base64-encoded, sensitive)' and provides generation command: 'Generation: Use `openssl rand -base64 32` to generate a secure 256-bit key'

claims.claim: JWT_SECRET must be at least 256 bits (32 bytes) for HMAC-SHA256 security
claims.evidence: Security section states 'Must be strong, random, and kept secret' and generation command 'openssl rand -base64 32' produces a 256-bit key. Document explicitly mentions 'secure 256-bit key'

claims.claim: Database user should have minimum required permissions (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)
claims.evidence: DB_USERNAME section states 'Security: Should have minimum required permissions (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on application tables)' and SECURITY CONSIDERATIONS reiterates 'The DB_USERNAME account should have minimal required permissions, not superuser access'

claims.claim: CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS should not use wildcards (*) in production
claims.evidence: CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS Important section states 'do NOT use wildcards (*) in production for security reasons' and 'Only list your actual frontend domains'

claims.claim: All 7 variables are required for production deployment
claims.evidence: DEPLOYMENT CHECKLIST section explicitly states 'Set all 7 required environment variables' and lists them in the Complete Environment Variables Checklist section

eval_feedback.overall: Strong, comprehensive assertions that verify both completeness (all variables listed) and depth (explanations provided, Base64 encoding mentioned). The assertions successfully discriminate between superficial listings and substantive explanations.
